I just delivered a meal to the wrong table and somehow they thanked me anyway.
Someone asked me what I'd recommend and then ordered the exact opposite.
The kitchen is calm. I'm suspicious.
A customer apologized for bothering me. They asked for a napkin.
I have walked enough steps tonight to cross a small country.
Someone waved me over just to say the food was amazing. That was nice.
A table spent ten minutes deciding who would ask for ketchup.
I've memorized three orders and forgotten where I put my pen.
A customer asked if I ever sit down. Rarely.
One table is celebrating. The other is negotiating. I don't know which is louder.
Someone asked for extra ice. Then asked me to remove the ice.
I can tell who's ready to order and who's pretending.
The dessert menu has caused more arguments than I'd expect.
A customer thanked me for smiling. I didn't realize I was.
Someone ordered the same thing as their friend and still wanted to compare plates.
I have delivered six baskets of bread and peace remains temporary.
A table just adopted me as their honorary family member for the evening.
Someone asked me for a recommendation and actually took it.
The busiest tables always say they're easy customers.
A customer dropped a fork and looked at me like it was a personal failure.
I've heard three birthday songs tonight and survived all of them.
Someone asked if the food was good. I work here.
A table spent twenty minutes choosing drinks and two seconds choosing food.
The children are full of energy. Their parents are not.
Someone complimented the service and I'm holding onto that for the rest of the week.
A customer asked if a dish was spicy. It depends how brave you're feeling.
The kitchen bell has become the soundtrack of my evening.
Someone changed their order four times. A personal record.
I've refilled the same glass three times in ten minutes.
A table just cheered because their food arrived. I appreciate the enthusiasm.
Someone asked for the bill and then immediately ordered dessert.
I know exactly which tables will ask for extra sauce.
A customer said they'd be quick. They were not.
The restaurant is full and somehow everyone needs me at once.
Someone thanked me for remembering their usual order.
A table asked for recommendations and then took notes.
The food is hot, the plates are heavy and the shift continues.
Someone asked me if we're busy. I laughed.
A customer said everything was perfect and honestly that made my day.
One table is discussing life goals. Another is debating french fries.
I've mastered the art of carrying too many plates at once.
Someone called me over and forgot why.
The bread basket has disappeared again.
A customer asked for a quiet table. Their friends arrived five minutes later.
I've delivered enough drinks tonight to fill a fountain.
Someone ordered dessert before their main course. I respect the confidence.
A table spent fifteen minutes taking photos of their food.
A customer said they'd only stay for a few minutes. They're still here.
The restaurant has two speeds: empty and chaos.
Someone asked me how my shift was going. That's rare.
A table just applauded when their friend finished a meal.
I've walked past the same chair six times and almost tripped every time.
Someone requested extra napkins. They knew what was coming.
A customer asked for a surprise dessert. The kitchen accepted the challenge.
The last hour of a shift somehow lasts the longest.
Someone complimented the food like I cooked it myself.
A table left happier than when they arrived. I like seeing that.
The dining room is finally getting quieter.
One more table, one more order, one more trip to the kitchen.
The chairs are being stacked, the lights are dimming and I survived another shift.
